WJEC CBAC
                                 GCE MARKING SCHEME
                                     Predicted Paper (Moderate)

                                     ECONOMICS – UNIT 3
                                              1520U30-1


About this marking scheme
This marking scheme has been produced in the style of the WJEC A-Level Economics Unit 3 mark
schemes. It reflects positive marking principles: credit should be given for what the learner writes,
rather than penalising omissions. A very good learner should be able to achieve full marks.
Learners must demonstrate their ability to:
 • AO1 — Demonstrate knowledge of terms/concepts and theories/models.
 • AO2 — Apply knowledge and understanding to various economic contexts.
 • AO3 — Analyse issues within economics, showing an understanding of their impact on economic
    agents.
 • AO4 — Evaluate economic arguments and use qualitative and quantitative evidence to support
    informed judgements.


                                            (1520U30-1) Page 1
GCE A LEVEL ECONOMICS – UNIT 3
PREDICTED PAPER (MODERATE) MARK SCHEME
Question 1 (a) — Internal economies vs internal diseconomies of scale (4 marks)
AO1 – 2 marks
 • Award 1 mark for understanding of the difference: economies of scale occur when average/unit
    costs fall as output rises, whereas diseconomies of scale occur when average/unit costs rise as
    output rises.
 • Award 1 mark for a correctly drawn LRAC curve (U-shaped) with output on the x-axis and costs on
    the y-axis. Do not accept 'AC' alone.
AO2 – 2 marks
 • Award 1 mark for identifying an Amazon internal economy of scale e.g. bulk-buying from suppliers,
    use of robotic technology reducing unit costs.
 • Award 1 mark for identifying an Amazon internal diseconomy of scale e.g. managers struggling to
    supervise, breakdown in communication, falling worker morale/rising staff turnover.

Question 1 (b) — External economies of scale in Swindon (4 marks)
AO1 – 2 marks
 • Award 1 mark for understanding external economies of scale — LRAC falls due to growth of the
    wider industry/area around the firm, not the firm itself.
 • Award 1 mark for a correct diagram showing a downward shift in the LRAC curve (LRAC₁ below
    LRAC).
AO2 – 2 marks
 • Award 1 mark for linking to the data e.g. suppliers and logistics firms setting up in Swindon, council
    improving road links.
 • Award 1 mark for linking to how Amazon's LRAC falls e.g. improved local transport infrastructure
    reduces delivery times and fuel costs; proximity of suppliers cuts inbound logistics costs.

Question 2 (a)(b)(c) — Monopolistically competitive firm diagram (7 marks)
(a) 1 mark: Candidate correctly identifies output where MC = MR (AO2).
(b) 2 marks: Candidate shades the rectangle bounded by the profit-maximising output, AR at that
output, and AC at that output (OFR allowed if output in (a) was wrong).
(c) 4 marks: Long-run adjustment.
 • AO1 (1 mark): Low barriers to entry in monopolistic competition allow new firms to enter if abnormal
    profit exists.
 • AO2 (1 mark): Recognises that this firm is currently earning abnormal/supernormal profit which
    attracts new entrants.
 • AO3 (2 marks): New firms entering cause the firm's demand (AR) and MR curves to shift left (and
    often become more elastic), reducing profit-maximising output. In the long run the firm earns only
    normal profit (AR = AC) at the new MR = MC output. Answers may illustrate this on the diagram.

Question 3 — Evaluate the privatisation of Royal Mail (8 marks)

Indicative content (in favour of success):
 • Government raised £2bn through the sale and relieved itself of ongoing loss-making operations.
 • Private investment enabled modernisation of parcel sorting (parcel automation mentioned in case).
 • Royal Mail able to compete with DPD/Amazon in the growing parcel market.
 • Employees received shares — wider share ownership.
Indicative content (against success):
 • Shares widely viewed as sold too cheaply — share price rose sharply on day one, costing taxpayers.
 • Share price has since fallen below the 2013 flotation price — questions about long-run value
    creation.
 • CWU strikes reflect industrial relations breakdown.
 • Ofcom fines for missed Universal Service Obligation (USO) delivery targets.
 • 2024 takeover by a foreign billionaire raises questions about strategic national infrastructure.
 • Principal-agent problem: shareholders prioritise dividends over service quality.
Note – this is a reversible answer (for band 2 marks).

Question 4 — Impact of higher interest rates on UK AD (6 marks)

                          and the exchange         from 0.25% to 5.25%).
                          rate.

           1              1 mark — Limited         1 mark — At least one    1 mark — Superficial
                          knowledge covering       reference to the data.   analysis referring
                          savers OR borrowers.                              only to AD as a


                                             (1520U30-1) Page 3
                                                                              whole.

            0             0 marks — No              0 marks — No              0 marks — No
                          relevant knowledge.       relevant application.     relevant analysis.

Indicative content:
 • C falls: higher cost of borrowing discourages credit-financed consumption (cars, home
    improvements). UK has relatively high household debt so marginal propensity to be affected is
    large.
 • C falls further through mortgage transmission: roughly a third of households have a mortgage; rising
    rates reduce disposable income.
 • I falls: higher cost of capital raises hurdle rates for business investment.
 • G: higher debt service costs squeeze government spending.
 • (X–M): higher UK rates attract hot money inflows, sterling appreciates, exports less competitive,
    imports cheaper — net trade worsens.
 • A rise of 500 basis points in 20 months is historically large and transmission lags of 12–18 months
    mean the full effect was still working through by 2024.

Question 5 (a) — Price index calculation (3 marks)
Weighted calculation method:
(19.1 × 11) + (26.1 × 27) + (7.2 × 7) + (4.9 × 31) + (6.3 × 18) + (−5.9 × 6) all divided by 100
= (210.1 + 704.7 + 50.4 + 151.9 + 113.4 − 35.4) / 100 = 1195.1 / 100 = 11.95%
Price index = 111.95 (accept 111.9 – 112.0)
 • AO3 (1 mark) for use of weighted index method (regardless of accuracy).
 • AO2 (2 marks) for correct answer.
 • Award 2 marks in total for 11.95% or 12% without expressing as an index.
 • If +5.9 used instead of −5.9, award 2 marks (answer 112.66/112.7).

Question 5 (b) — Assess wage-price spiral in the UK (8 marks)
         Band                     AO2 (2)                  AO3 (2)                     AO4 (4)

                                                                              evaluation,

                          two Figures.              a developed chain of      argument.

            1             1 mark — Data             1 mark — Superficial      1 mark — Limited

                          or on one side.                                     counter not

            0             0                         0                         0

                                             (1520U30-1) Page 4
Indicative content (in favour of spiral):
 • Figure 1 shows CPI reached 9.1% in 2022, well above the 2% target — workers will expect pay rises
    to protect real incomes.
 • Figure 2 shows essential categories (food 19.1%, energy 26.1%, rent 4.9%) rose sharply. Because
    rent, food and energy together make up 69% of spending weighting, their rises feed strongly into
    headline CPI.
 • Figure 3 shows nominal wages rising (84 in 2019 to 100 in 2023, +19%) — firms may pass these
    increases into higher prices.
 • Tight labour market/high vacancies during 2022–23 gave workers bargaining power.
 • Textbook mechanism: higher CPI → pay demands → higher unit labour costs → SRAS shifts left →
    higher CPI…
Indicative content (against spiral):
 • Figure 3 shows real wages fell from 104 (2019) to 100 (2023) — workers have absorbed losses
    rather than won full compensation, breaking the spiral.
 • Bank of England raised rates aggressively to 5.25% to anchor expectations (Figure 1, Q4).
 • CPI fell to 2.8% by 2024 (Figure 1), suggesting inflationary psychology did not take hold.
 • Inflation was largely cost-push (energy, food import prices) rather than wage-driven — a monetary
    policy response, not a wage-spiral dynamic.
 • Unionisation in the private sector is historically low (~13%), limiting wage bargaining power.
Note – this is a reversible answer.


                                            (1520U30-1) Page 5
Section B — UK Cost of Living Crisis
Question 6 (a)(i) — % change in gas price (2 marks)
(1.10 − 0.25) / 0.25 × 100 = 85/25 × 100 = 340% (1 mark for method, 1 mark for correct answer).
Accept 340%.

Question 6 (a)(ii) — Percentage change in total commodity cost (2 marks)
2020 cost: (500 × £155) + (100 × £180) = £77,500 + £18,000 = £95,500
2023 cost: (500 × £230) + (100 × £560) = £115,000 + £56,000 = £171,000
% change = (£171,000 − £95,500) / £95,500 × 100 = £75,500 / £95,500 × 100 ≈ 79.1%
Accept 79% to 79.1%. Award 1 mark for correct method, 1 mark for correct final answer.

Indicative AO1 content:
 • MC and AC shift upwards (MC₂, AC₂) — 1 mark.
 • New accurate lower output at MR = MC₂ (Q₂) — 1 mark.
 • New accurate higher price (P₂) — 1 mark.
 • New accurate lower abnormal profit area — 1 mark.
AO2 — data links:
 • Natural gas up 340%, fertiliser prices more than tripled (Table 1).
 • Wheat up nearly 50% — raises costs for UK bakeries/pub food.
 • UK Bank Rate rising to 5.25% (Figure 2) simultaneously reduces demand — AR shifts left too,
    worsening profit squeeze.

Question 7 — External vs internal factors in UK crisis (8 marks)

                         well on both sides.      developed chains of        developed counter-

           1             1 mark — Data used       1 mark — Limited           1 mark — Limited
                         superficially or on      chains of reasoning.       evaluation.
                         one side only.

           0             0                        0                          0

External factors:
 • War in Ukraine pushed European gas prices to record highs (gas up 340% in Table 1).
 • Global supply chain disruption post-COVID raised import prices.
 • Global food commodity inflation driven by weather and fertiliser costs.
 • US monetary tightening raised dollar-priced import costs.
Internal factors:
 • Brexit-related trade frictions raised import costs and reduced labour supply.
 • UK's poor productivity record (under 1% growth since 2008 — Table 2) limits real wage growth.
 • Heavy reliance on gas-fired heating (~40% of homes) reflects long-term policy neglect of home
    insulation.
 • Bank of England arguably too slow to raise interest rates in 2021.
 • Fiscal response (Energy Price Guarantee, £40bn) partly fuelled demand-side inflation.
This is a reversible answer for Band 2.

Question 8 — Should Bank of England have intervened more aggressively? (10
marks)
         Band                    AO2 (4)                  AO3 (3)                   AO4 (3)

           3             4 marks — Data           3 marks — Excellent        3 marks — Well-
                         embedded                 analysis across both       rounded judgement
                         throughout on both       proposed                   referring to both
                         sides, covering both     interventions (earlier     forms of intervention.
                         forms of intervention.   rate rises AND faster
                                                  QT).

           2             2–3 marks — Data         2 marks — Well-            2 marks — Well-
                         used well on both        developed analysis         developed counter-
                         sides, may cover only    but narrow range.          argument.

                                            (1520U30-1) Page 7
                         one intervention in
                         depth.

           1             1 mark — Limited use        1 mark — Limited        1 mark — Limited
                         of data.                    analysis.               evaluation.

           0             0                           0                       0

Support more aggressive intervention:
 • CPI hit 10.5% in 2022 (Fig. 2), five times the target — earlier tightening could have anchored
    expectations.
 • Real interest rates were deeply negative for much of 2022, effectively stimulating demand during an
    inflationary shock.
 • QT more quickly would have reduced the £895bn balance sheet and tightened financial conditions
   faster.
 • Credibility risk: repeated above-target outcomes damage the Bank's inflation-fighting reputation.
Against more aggressive intervention:
 • Inflation was largely cost-push (imported energy and food) — monetary policy cannot directly fix a
    supply shock.
 • Two million fixed-rate mortgages reset in 2023–24; more rapid rises would have caused serious
    financial distress.
 • Over-tightening risked a deep recession and a rise in unemployment.
 • By 2024 CPI returned to 2.8% (Fig. 2) — suggesting existing policy worked, if slowly.
 • Faster QT could have destabilised the gilt market (recall the 2022 LDI crisis).
Reversible answer. If only one intervention is evaluated, maximum Band 2 (7/10).

Question 9 — Will industrial strategy improve UK living standards? (12 marks)
         Band                    AO2 (4)                     AO3 (3)                 AO4 (5)

           3             4 marks — Case              3 marks — Well-         5 marks — Well-
                         embedded                    developed analysis      rounded judgement
                         throughout.                 with clear focus on     on whether industrial
                                                     living standards        strategy will improve
                                                     indicators (Table 2).   living standards.

           2             2–3 marks — Case            2 marks — Well-         3–4 marks — Well-
                         well used but uneven.       developed analysis      developed counter-
                                                     with narrower           arguments.
                                                     range/less focus on
                                                     LS.

           1             1 mark — Superficial        1 mark — Limited        1–2 marks — Limited
                         case use.                   analysis.               evaluation.

           0             0                           0                       0

In favour of improved living standards:
 • Investment in nuclear/offshore wind reduces gas import dependence and insulates from future
    energy shocks (case identifies 40% gas-heating vulnerability).


                                               (1520U30-1) Page 8
 • Green-transition retraining tackles skills shortages, raises productivity (Table 2 shows UK at 62.5 vs
   G7 82.1).
 • Higher productivity enables real wage growth, raising real disposable income (Fig. 1 shows flatlining
    since 2019).
 • Closer EU trade relations reduce trade frictions → lower import prices, better export markets.
 • A clear industrial strategy provides the policy certainty that business investment needs.
Against:
 • UK has a poor track record of industrial strategy — previous attempts have been reversed by
    successive governments.
 • Public debt already over 100% of GDP (Table 2) — limited fiscal space for large industrial spending.
 • Regional inequality (Gini 0.355) may persist because investment often concentrates in the South
    East.
 • Green-transition costs may raise energy prices in the short run, squeezing real incomes.
 • Deeper problems — low R&D spend, planning system rigidities, underinvestment since 2008 — are
    hard to fix.
 • Living standards also depend on public services already weakened by a decade of restraint.
For Band 3 responses there needs to be a clear focus on living standards and how industrial strategy
affects LS indicators.
